description: This figure presents the dynamic recrystallization (DRX) average grain size of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density high-strength steel as a function of deformation temperature (900–1150°C) under four different strain rates: 0.01 s⁻¹ (black squares), 0.1 s⁻¹ (red circles), 1 s⁻¹ (blue triangles), and 10 s⁻¹ (magenta stars). The data points represent measurements taken from the core region of compressed samples at 60% height reduction. All curves demonstrate a consistent trend where DRX grain size increases monotonically with decreasing strain rate and increasing temperature. Specifically, at 1150°C and 0.01 s⁻¹, the grain size exceeds 70 μm (approximately 75 μm), indicating rapid grain growth and coarse microstructure formation. Conversely, at lower temperatures (900–950°C) and higher strain rates (1–10 s⁻¹), the DRX grains remain relatively small (approximately 4–22 μm) but incomplete, with retained original coarse grains resulting in poor microstructural homogeneity. The figure experimentally validates the grain size prediction model obtained via Levenberg-Marquardt optimization, confirming that the steel exhibits positive temperature sensitivity and negative strain-rate sensitivity for DRX grain growth, which provides critical guidance for optimizing hot working parameters to achieve fine and uniform grain structures.
